title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: psvr R package implementing four SVR models derived unified mathematical framework percentage-error loss functions. targets forecasting contexts targets yk > 0 strictly positive relative accuracy matters absolute accuracy. Source paper: Unified Family Percentage-Error Support Vector Regression Models Symmetric Kernel Extensions (MDPI Mathematics, 2026). Full derivations paper.tex repository root.

what: Model 1 — ε-SVR with MAPE (Theorem 1 / Appendix A.1)
title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: Primal: Dual QP (max form): βk = αk - αk*, |βk| = αk + αk*. KKT: ω = Σk βk φ(xk), Σk βk = 0, 0 ≤ αk, αk* ≤ 100C/yk. Prediction: f(x) = Σk βk K(xk, x) + b Implementation: QP via osqp. Key files: R/mape_svr.R.
code: min  ½‖ω‖² + C Σk (ξk + ξk*) s.t. (100/yk)(yk - ωᵀφ(xk) - b) ≤ ε + ξk      (100/yk)(ωᵀφ(xk) + b - yk) ≤ ε + ξk*      ξk, ξk* ≥ 0 max  -½ Σk,l βk βl K(xk,xl) + Σk βk yk - (ε/100) Σk |βk| yk s.t. Σk βk = 0      |βk| ≤ 100C / yk    (sample-dependent box; yk small → tight bound)

what: Model 2 — Symmetric ε-SVR with MAPE (Theorem 2 / Appendix A.2)
title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: primal Model 1, augmented symmetry constraint: ωᵀφ(xk) = · ωᵀφ(-xk) Dual QP (max form): Ks(xi,xj) = K(xi,xj) + ·K(xi,-xj). factor ¼ (vs ½ Model 1) arises symmetrization representer theorem (Appendix .2): ωᵀφ(xk) = ½ Σl βl Ks(xl, xk), quadratic term picks extra ½. Prediction: f(x) = ½ Σk βk Ks(xk, x) + b Implementation: QP via osqp. Build Ks = Ω + ·Ω* inline (½), pass P = ¼·Ks QP quadratic matrix — ¼ factor absorbed P directly. use sym_kernel_matrix() ; helper returns ½(Ω + ·Ω*) = Ωs, correct Model 4. Key files: R/mape_sym_svr.R.
code: max  -¼ Σk,l βk βl Ks(xk,xl) + Σk βk yk - (ε/100) Σk |βk| yk s.t. Σk βk = 0      |βk| ≤ 100C / yk    (unchanged from Model 1)

what: Model 3 — LS-SVR with RMSPE (Theorem 3 / Appendix A.3)
title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: Primal: KKT stationarity: - ω = Σk αk φ(xk) - Σk αk = 0 - ek = (yk²/Γ) αk Linear system (solve directly): YΓ = diag(y1²/Γ, …, yN²/Γ) — added diagonal Ω . Prediction: f(x) = Σk αk K(xk, x) + b Implementation: base::solve() (N+1)×(N+1) augmented matrix. Key files: R/rmspe_lssvr.R.
code: min  ½‖ω‖² + (Γ/2) Σk ek²/yk² s.t. yk = ωᵀφ(xk) + b + ek [ 0    1ᵀ      ] [b]   [0] [ 1    Ω + YΓ  ] [α] = [y]

what: Model 4 — Symmetric LS-SVR with RMSPE (Theorem 4 / Appendix A.4)
title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: primal Model 3, augmented symmetry constraint. Linear system: Ωs = ½(Ω + aΩ*), Ω*kl = K(xk, -xl). Symmetric representer: ωᵀφ(xk) = Σl (Ωs)kl αl Prediction: f(x) = ½ Σk αk Ks(xk, x) + b equivalently: f(x) = Σk αk [K(xk,x) + ·K(xk,-x)] / 2 + b Implementation: base::solve() Ωs replacing Ω. Key files: R/rmspe_sym_lssvr.R.

title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: Required Models 2 4: - K(-xi, xj) = K(xi, -xj) - K(-xi, -xj) = K(xi, xj) RBF kernel K(xi, xj) = exp(-‖xi - xj‖²/2σ²) satisfies conditions: - K(-xi, -xj) = exp(-‖-xi-(-xj)‖²/2σ²) = exp(-‖xi-xj‖²/2σ²) = K(xi,xj) ✓ - K(-xi, xj) = exp(-‖-xi-xj‖²/2σ²) = exp(-‖xi+xj‖²/2σ²) = K(xi,-xj) ✓ Polynomial kernels even degree also satisfy Assumption 3.

title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: QP solver: osqp package (Models 1 & 2). Supports sparse matrices, commercial license. Linear solver: base::solve() (Models 3 & 4). Dense (N+1)×(N+1) system, extra dep. Target validation: fit() functions call stopifnot((y > 0)). Kernel interface: make_kernel(type, ...) returns closure function(xi, xj) type ∈ {"rbf", "linear", "polynomial"}. Shared across four models. YΓ storage: stored vector y^2 / gamma; added diagonal via diag(Omega) <- diag(Omega) + y_gamma — never materializes full N×N diagonal matrix. "psvr_mape" (Model 1) "psvr_mape_sym" (Model 2) "psvr_rmspe" (Model 3) "psvr_rmspe_sym" (Model 4) S3 methods: predict.psvr_*() class. Documentation: roxygen2 @param, @return, @examples. Style: tidyverse / base R style: snake_case, <- assignment. License: MIT.

title: psvr — Percentage-error Support Vector Regression
text: yk > 0 always — validated fit time, never silently coerced. ∈ {-1, 1} symmetric models. symmetric kernel Ks(xi, xj) = K(xi, xj) + ·K(xi, -xj) uses negation xj, kernel closure must accept negative inputs even training data positive. YΓ diagonal — add diag(Omega) place, never build N×N diagonal matrix. Box constraints Models 1 & 2 per-sample: |βk| ≤ 100C/yk. Prediction Model 2: multiply ½ (symmetric representer theorem). Prediction Model 4: use ½(K(xk,x) + ·K(xk,-x)) per support vector.

title: Electricity Demand Forecasting with the modeltime + psvr Stack
text: Victoria, Australia operates competitive electricity spot market forecast errors translate directly monetary penalties, making relative accuracy natural objective. vic_elec dataset tsibbledata records half-hourly demand temperature readings 2012–2014. aggregate daily level. Figure 1: Victoria daily electricity demand, 2012–2014. Summer heat-wave spikes lower weekend/holiday baseline clearly visible.
code: elec_daily <- vic_elec |>   as_tibble() |>   mutate(Date = as_date(Time)) |>   group_by(Date) |>   summarise(     Demand      = sum(Demand) / 1e3,   # GWh/day — better scale for SVR     Temperature = mean(Temperature, na.rm = TRUE),     IsHoliday   = any(Holiday),     .groups     = "drop"   ) |>   mutate(IsHoliday = as.integer(IsHoliday))  cat(sprintf(   "Rows: %d  |  Dates: %s – %s\n",   nrow(elec_daily), min(elec_daily$Date), max(elec_daily$Date) )) Rows: 1096  |  Dates: 2012-01-01 – 2014-12-31 elec_daily |>   plot_time_series(     Date, Demand,     .title       = "Victoria daily electricity demand",     .y_lab       = "Demand (GWh/day)",     .interactive = FALSE   )

title: Electricity Demand Forecasting with the modeltime + psvr Stack

splits <- time_series_split(   elec_daily,   date_var   = Date,   assess     = "1 year",   cumulative = TRUE )  train_raw <- training(splits) test_raw  <- testing(splits)  # Scale targets to ~[0.7, 1.6] for numerical conditioning # MAPE and RMSPE are scale-invariant so metrics are unaffected y_scale <- mean(train_raw$Demand)  train <- train_raw |> mutate(Demand = Demand / y_scale) test  <- test_raw  |> mutate(Demand = Demand / y_scale)  # Scaled version of full series for forecast plot elec_daily_scaled <- elec_daily |> mutate(Demand = Demand / y_scale)  cat(sprintf("Train: %d days  (%s – %s)
",             nrow(train), min(train$Date), max(train$Date))) Train: 730 days  (2012-01-01 – 2013-12-30) cat(sprintf("Test:  %d days  (%s – %s)
",             nrow(test),  min(test$Date),  max(test$Date))) Test:  366 days  (2013-12-31 – 2014-12-31) cat(sprintf("Scaled demand range (train): %.3f – %.3f
",             min(train$Demand), max(train$Demand))) Scaled demand range (train): 0.715 – 1.382

text: step_timeseries_signature() automatically extracts calendar features (year, quarter, month, week, day--week, etc.) Date column, replacing need manual mutate() calls. bake training set compute data-driven rbf_sigma search range four psvr models.
code: rec <- recipe(Demand ~ ., data = train) |>   update_role(Date, new_role = "ID") |>   step_timeseries_signature(Date) |>   step_rm(matches("(iso$|xts$|hour|minute|second|am.pm)")) |>   step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors()) |>   step_dummy(all_nominal_predictors()) train_baked      <- rec |> prep() |> bake(new_data = train) rbf_sigma_custom <- rbf_sigma_psvr_data(   train_baked |> select(-Demand, -Date) )  r <- dials::range_get(rbf_sigma_custom, original = FALSE) cat(sprintf("rbf_sigma search range (log10): [%.3f, %.3f]\n", r$lower, r$upper)) rbf_sigma search range (log10): [-0.222, 1.778]

title: Electricity Demand Forecasting with the modeltime + psvr Stack
text: Model formulations mathematical derivations four models, see Benavides-Herrera et al. (2026).
code: # psvr models spec_m1 <- psvr_mape_rbf(   cost = tune(), svm_margin = tune(), rbf_sigma = tune() ) |> set_engine("psvr")  spec_m2 <- psvr_mape_sym_rbf(   cost = tune(), svm_margin = tune(), rbf_sigma = tune() ) |> set_engine("psvr")  spec_m3 <- psvr_rmspe_rbf(   cost = tune(), rbf_sigma = tune() ) |> set_engine("psvr")  spec_m4 <- psvr_rmspe_sym_rbf(   cost = tune(), rbf_sigma = tune() ) |> set_engine("psvr")  # Baselines spec_ksvm <- svm_rbf(cost = tune(), rbf_sigma = tune()) |>   set_engine("kernlab") |>   set_mode("regression")  spec_lm <- linear_reg() |>   set_engine("lm")  spec_rf <- rand_forest(mtry = tune(), trees = 500, min_n = tune()) |>   set_engine("randomForest") |>   set_mode("regression")

title: Electricity Demand Forecasting with the modeltime + psvr Stack
code: wf_set <- workflow_set(   preproc = list(base = rec),   models  = list(     lm           = spec_lm,     ksvm         = spec_ksvm,     rf           = spec_rf,     m1_mape      = spec_m1,     m2_mape_sym  = spec_m2,     m3_rmspe     = spec_m3,     m4_rmspe_sym = spec_m4   ) ) |>   psvr_option_add(train_baked |> select(-Demand, -Date))  wf_set # A workflow set/tibble: 7 × 4   wflow_id          info             option    result   <chr>             <list>           <list>    <list> 1 base_lm           <tibble [1 × 4]> <opts[0]> <list [0]> 2 base_ksvm         <tibble [1 × 4]> <opts[0]> <list [0]> 3 base_rf           <tibble [1 × 4]> <opts[0]> <list [0]> 4 base_m1_mape      <tibble [1 × 4]> <opts[1]> <list [0]> 5 base_m2_mape_sym  <tibble [1 × 4]> <opts[1]> <list [0]> 6 base_m3_rmspe     <tibble [1 × 4]> <opts[1]> <list [0]> 7 base_m4_rmspe_sym <tibble [1 × 4]> <opts[1]> <list [0]> # Linear model has no tunable params — run fit_resamples on its subset wf_lm    <- wf_set[wf_set$wflow_id == "base_lm",  ] wf_other <- wf_set[wf_set$wflow_id != "base_lm",  ]  lm_res <- workflow_map(   wf_lm, "fit_resamples",   resamples = folds,   metrics   = metric_set(mape, rmse, rsq),   control   = control_resamples(save_pred = FALSE, verbose = FALSE) )  plan(multisession) set.seed(123) tune_res_other <- workflow_map(   wf_other, "tune_grid",   resamples  = folds,   grid       = 5,   metrics    = metric_set(mape, rmse, rsq),   control    = control_grid(     save_pred     = FALSE,     parallel_over = "everything",     verbose       = FALSE   ) ) plan(sequential)  # Combine — bind_rows preserves the workflow_set class tune_res <- dplyr::bind_rows(lm_res, tune_res_other)

text: Figure 3: Cross-validated MAPE 7 workflows (lower better). Error bars show ±1.96 SE. finalize every workflow best hyperparameters, fit full training set, use modeltime_accuracy() unified test-set comparison. Table 1: Test-set accuracy 7 models, sorted MAPE (ascending). Figure 4: Test-set MAPE vs. RMSE 7 models. Models lower-left quadrant perform well metrics simultaneously. Note models optimising different losses occupy distinct regions space. Figure 5: Test-set forecasts 7 models vs. actual daily demand. Use range slider zoom specific periods.
code: rank_res <- rank_results(tune_res, rank_metric = "mape", select_best = TRUE)  rank_res |>   filter(.metric == "mape") |>   mutate(     wflow_id = fct_reorder(wflow_id, mean),     family   = case_when(       str_detect(wflow_id, "m1|m2|m3|m4") ~ "psvr",       str_detect(wflow_id, "ksvm")         ~ "SVM (kernlab)",       str_detect(wflow_id, "rf")           ~ "Random Forest",       TRUE                                  ~ "Linear"     )   ) |>   ggplot(aes(x = mean, y = wflow_id, colour = family, shape = family)) +   geom_point(size = 3) +   geom_errorbarh(     aes(xmin = mean - 1.96 * std_err, xmax = mean + 1.96 * std_err),     height = 0.25   ) +   scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set1") +   labs(     x        = "Cross-validated MAPE (%)",     y        = NULL,     colour   = "Model family",     shape    = "Model family",     title    = "Model comparison: cross-validated MAPE",     subtitle = "Points show best result per workflow; bars show ±1.96 SE"   ) all_wf_ids <- tune_res$wflow_id  all_final_fits <- map(all_wf_ids, \(wf_id) {   res <- tune_res |> extract_workflow_set_result(wf_id)   wf  <- tune_res |> extract_workflow(wf_id)    # select_best only applies to tune_grid results; lm used fit_resamples   if (inherits(res, "tune_results")) {     best_p <- select_best(res, metric = "mape")     wf <- finalize_workflow(wf, best_p)   }    fit(wf, train) }) mt_tbl <- do.call(modeltime_table, all_final_fits) |>   mutate(.model_desc = str_remove(all_wf_ids, "^base_"))  calib_tbl <- mt_tbl |>   modeltime_calibrate(new_data = test)  # Standard accuracy metrics accuracy_tbl <- calib_tbl |>   modeltime_accuracy()  # RMSPE — computed manually from calibrated forecasts rmspe_tbl <- calib_tbl |>   modeltime_forecast(new_data = test, actual_data = elec_daily) |>   filter(.key == "prediction") |>   left_join(     elec_daily |> select(Date, Demand),     by = c(".index" = "Date")   ) |>   group_by(.model_desc) |>   summarise(     rmspe = sqrt(mean(((.value - Demand) / Demand)^2)) * 100,     .groups = "drop"   )  # Join and display accuracy_tbl |>   left_join(rmspe_tbl, by = ".model_desc") |>   arrange(mape) |>   select(.model_desc, mae, mape, rmspe, rmse, rsq) |>   kable(     digits    = 3,     col.names = c("Model", "MAE", "MAPE (%)", "RMSPE (%)", "RMSE", "RSQ")   ) accuracy_tbl |>   mutate(     family = case_when(       str_detect(.model_desc, "m1|m2|m3|m4") ~ "psvr",       str_detect(.model_desc, "ksvm")         ~ "SVM (kernlab)",       str_detect(.model_desc, "rf")           ~ "Random Forest",       TRUE                                     ~ "Linear"     ),     label = .model_desc   ) |>   ggplot(aes(x = rmse, y = mape, colour = family, label = label)) +   geom_point(size = 3.5, alpha = 0.85) +   ggrepel::geom_text_repel(size = 3, max.overlaps = 20) +   scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set1") +   labs(     x        = "Test-set RMSE (GWh/day, normalised)",     y        = "Test-set MAPE (%)",     colour   = "Model family",     title    = "MAPE vs. RMSE trade-off",     subtitle = "Lower-left = better on both metrics"   ) calib_tbl |>   modeltime_forecast(     new_data    = test,     actual_data = elec_daily_scaled   ) |>   mutate(     .model_desc = case_when(       .key == "actual"    ~ "Actual",       TRUE                ~ str_remove(.model_desc, "^base_")     )   ) |>   plot_modeltime_forecast(     .legend_show      = TRUE,     .title            = "All models: test-set forecast vs actual",     .interactive      = TRUE,     .plotly_slider    = TRUE   )

title: Getting Started with psvr
text: Classical SVR minimises absolute-error losses (MAE, MSE), misaligned scale-free accuracy criteria standard forecasting. error 1 unit negligible target 1 000 large 2. psvr implements four SVR variants derived percentage-error loss functions (Benavides-Herrera et al., 2026): models require strictly positive targets (y > 0), condition percentage residuals well-defined.

title: Getting Started with psvr
code: # CRAN (once released) install.packages("psvr")  # Development version from GitHub remotes::install_github("pbenavidesh/psvr")

title: Getting Started with psvr
text: reproduce experiments reported Tables 1–2 paper using Boston Housing dataset (Harrison & Rubinfeld, 1978). 506 observations strictly positive median home values (MEDV, thousands USD), making MAPE well-defined throughout.
code: library(psvr) library(ggplot2)  # Boston Housing is available in the MASS package data("Boston", package = "MASS")  # Target: median home value (all > 0) y_all <- Boston$medv X_raw <- as.matrix(Boston[, setdiff(names(Boston), "medv")])  stopifnot(all(y_all > 0)) cat("N =", nrow(X_raw), "  p =", ncol(X_raw),     "  y range: [", round(min(y_all), 1), ",", round(max(y_all), 1), "]\n") #> N = 506   p = 13   y range: [ 5 , 50 ]

title: Getting Started with psvr
text: Features standardised using training-set statistics RBF kernel operates comparable scale across 13 predictors.
code: set.seed(42) n      <- nrow(X_raw) tr_idx <- sample(n, floor(0.7 * n))  X_raw_tr <- X_raw[tr_idx, ];  y_tr <- y_all[tr_idx] X_raw_te <- X_raw[-tr_idx, ]; y_te <- y_all[-tr_idx]  # Standardise: centre and scale by training mean/sd col_mean <- colMeans(X_raw_tr) col_sd   <- apply(X_raw_tr, 2, sd) X_tr <- scale(X_raw_tr, center = col_mean, scale = col_sd) X_te <- scale(X_raw_te, center = col_mean, scale = col_sd)

title: Getting Started with psvr
code: mape  <- function(y, yhat) mean(abs(y - yhat) / y) * 100 rmspe <- function(y, yhat) sqrt(mean(((y - yhat) / y)^2)) * 100 r2    <- function(y, yhat) 1 - sum((y - yhat)^2) / sum((y - mean(y))^2)

title: Getting Started with psvr
code: lm_df_tr <- as.data.frame(X_tr) lm_df_te <- as.data.frame(X_te) lm_fit   <- lm(y_tr ~ ., data = lm_df_tr) lm_pred  <- predict(lm_fit, newdata = lm_df_te)  cat(sprintf("Linear regression — MAPE: %.2f%%  RMSPE: %.2f%%  R²: %.4f\n",             mape(y_te, lm_pred), rmspe(y_te, lm_pred), r2(y_te, lm_pred))) #> Linear regression — MAPE: 16.29%  RMSPE: 22.48%  R²: 0.7730

title: Getting Started with psvr
text: LS-SVR formulation replaces QP linear system using quadratic penalty percentage residuals. dual reduces : [0𝟏⊤𝟏Ω+YΓ][b𝛂]=[0𝐲] \begin{bmatrix} 0 & \mathbf{1}^\top \\ \mathbf{1} & \Omega + Y_\Gamma \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} b \\ \boldsymbol{\alpha} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \mathbf{y} \end{bmatrix} YΓ=diag(y12/Γ,…,yN2/Γ)Y_\Gamma = \operatorname{diag}(y_1^2/\Gamma, \ldots, y_N^2/\Gamma).
code: # make_kernel() returns a closure K(xi, xj) = exp(-||xi - xj||^2 / (2 sigma^2)) K <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = 1)  # gamma = 5000: regularisation; larger gamma -> smaller Y_Gamma diagonal -> tighter fit fit_ls <- rmspe_lssvr(X_tr, y_tr, kernel = K, gamma = 5000) pred_ls <- predict(fit_ls, X_te)  cat(sprintf("LS-SVR RMSPE  — MAPE: %.2f%%  RMSPE: %.2f%%  R²: %.4f\n",             mape(y_te, pred_ls), rmspe(y_te, pred_ls), r2(y_te, pred_ls))) #> LS-SVR RMSPE  — MAPE: 15.46%  RMSPE: 27.21%  R²: 0.7277 print(fit_ls) #>  #> LS-SVR with RMSPE loss  [psvr_rmspe] #>  #>   Kernel:        RBF (sigma = 1) #>   Gamma:         5000 #>   Training obs.: 354 cf_ls <- coef(fit_ls) # alpha: N dual variables; weight each training point's kernel contribution #        in f(x) = sum_k alpha_k K(x_k, x) + b (all N points, no sparsity) # b:     bias / intercept term # X_sv:  all N training inputs stored for prediction cat(sprintf("b = %.4f  |  alpha range: [%.4f, %.4f]\n",             cf_ls$b, min(cf_ls$alpha), max(cf_ls$alpha))) #> b = 22.5268  |  alpha range: [-59.7517, 43.7321]

title: Getting Started with psvr
text: ε-SVR formulation optimises QP sample-dependent box constraints |βk|≤100C/yk|\beta_k| \le 100C/y_k: tighter bounds small targets, concentrating model capacity low-magnitude observations.
code:
# C = 10: per-sample box bound |beta_k| <= 100*C/y_k; eps = 1: tube width (% of y_k) fit_ep <- mape_svr(X_tr, y_tr, kernel = K, C = 10, eps = 1) pred_ep <- predict(fit_ep, X_te)  cat(sprintf("ε-SVR MAPE    — MAPE: %.2f%%  RMSPE: %.2f%%  R²: %.4f
",             mape(y_te, pred_ep), rmspe(y_te, pred_ep), r2(y_te, pred_ep))) #> ε-SVR MAPE    — MAPE: 15.77%  RMSPE: 27.54%  R²: 0.7617 cat(sprintf("Support vectors: %d / %d
", length(fit_ep$beta), nrow(X_tr))) #> Support vectors: 328 / 354 print(fit_ep) #>  #> Epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss  [psvr_mape] #>  #>   Kernel:          RBF (sigma = 1) #>   C:               10 #>   eps:             1 #>   Training obs.:   354 #>   Support vectors: 328 (92.7%) cf_ep <- coef(fit_ep) # alpha: beta_k = alpha_k - alpha_k* for each support vector; non-zero only for #        training points outside the percentage-error ε-tube (sparse) # b:     bias / intercept term # X_sv:  training rows corresponding to support vectors only cat(sprintf("b = %.4f  |  alpha range: [%.4f, %.4f]
",             cf_ep$b, min(cf_ep$alpha), max(cf_ep$alpha))) #> b = 23.1097  |  alpha range: [-111.5496, 82.6446]

title: Getting Started with psvr
text: Test-set performance Boston Housing (70/30 split, RBF kernel, single run). psvr models improve linear baseline respective percentage-error objectives. LS-SVR formulation (Model 3) minimises RMSPE directly achieves lowest squared-percentage error; ε-SVR formulation (Model 1) targets MAPE sample-dependent box constraints dual variables. results consistent Tables 1–2 Benavides-Herrera et al. (2026), SVR-MAPE reduces test-set MAPE 15.23% (classical ε-SVR) around 10% relative linear baseline 17.85%.
code: results <- data.frame(   Model = c("Linear regression", "LS-SVR RMSPE (Model 3)",             "\u03b5-SVR MAPE (Model 1)"),   MAPE  = c(mape(y_te,  lm_pred),             mape(y_te,  pred_ls),             mape(y_te,  pred_ep)),   RMSPE = c(rmspe(y_te, lm_pred),             rmspe(y_te, pred_ls),             rmspe(y_te, pred_ep)),   R2    = c(r2(y_te,    lm_pred),             r2(y_te,    pred_ls),             r2(y_te,    pred_ep)) ) results[, 2:4] <- round(results[, 2:4], 2) knitr::kable(results, col.names = c("Model", "MAPE (%)", "RMSPE (%)", "R²"),              align = "lrrr",              caption = paste("Test-set performance on Boston Housing",                              "(70/30 split, RBF kernel, single run)."))

title: Python Replication: Numerical Equivalence Check
text: vignette verifies R implementations psvr reproduce numerical results reported Tables 1–2 Benavides-Herrera et al. (2026), originally computed Python using cvxopt (Models 1 & 2) numpy.linalg.lstsq (Models 3 & 4). verification strategy : Use dataset, split seed, hyperparameters Python experiments. Report four metrics: MAPE, RMSPE, R², MSE test set. Compare R outputs paper reference values. Small numerical differences expected acceptable: Python implementation uses cvxopt QP (Models 1 & 2) pseudoinverse via numpy.linalg.lstsq linear system (Models 3 & 4), whereas psvr uses osqp base::solve(), respectively. approaches solve mathematical problem use different numerical algorithms different stopping tolerances, can produce discrepancies order 0.01–0.1 percentage points reported metrics.

title: Python Replication: Numerical Equivalence Check

title: Python Replication: Numerical Equivalence Check
text: Hyperparameters Table 1 Benavides-Herrera et al. (2026): Python RBF kernel uses gamma_py convention K(𝐱i,𝐱j)=exp(−γpy∥𝐱i−𝐱j∥2)K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp(-\gamma_{\text{py}} \|\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j\|^2), equivalent psvr RBF convention K(𝐱i,𝐱j)=exp(−∥𝐱i−𝐱j∥2/2σ2)K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp(-\|\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j\|^2 / 2\sigma^2) σ=1/(2γpy)\sigma = \sqrt{1 / (2\,\gamma_{\text{py}})}.
code:
sigma_m3 <- sqrt(1 / (2 * 0.050967)) cat("Model 3 — sigma:", round(sigma_m3, 6), "
") #> Model 3 — sigma: 3.132135  K3  <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = sigma_m3) fit_m3 <- rmspe_lssvr(X_tr, y_tr, kernel = K3, gamma = 16962.540770) pred_m3 <- predict(fit_m3, X_te)  m3_mape  <- mape_fn(y_te, pred_m3) m3_rmspe <- rmspe_fn(y_te, pred_m3) m3_r2    <- r2_fn(y_te, pred_m3) m3_mse   <- mse_fn(y_te, pred_m3)  cat(sprintf(   "Model 3 (R) — MAPE: %.4f%%  RMSPE: %.4f%%  R²: %.6f  MSE: %.6f
",   m3_mape, m3_rmspe, m3_r2, m3_mse )) #> Model 3 (R) — MAPE: 11.0495%  RMSPE: 18.7205%  R²: 0.819584  MSE: 14.127632 print(fit_m3) #>  #> LS-SVR with RMSPE loss  [psvr_rmspe] #>  #>   Kernel:        RBF (sigma = 3.13213) #>   Gamma:         16962.5 #>   Training obs.: 354 cf_m3 <- coef(fit_m3) # alpha: N dual variables; weight each training point in f(x) = sum_k alpha_k K(x_k, x) + b # b:     bias / intercept term # X_sv:  all N training inputs (LS-SVR — no sparsity, every point contributes) cat(sprintf("b = %.4f  |  alpha range: [%.4f, %.4f]
",             cf_m3$b, min(cf_m3$alpha), max(cf_m3$alpha))) #> b = 25.1360  |  alpha range: [-774.4447, 307.9511]

title: Python Replication: Numerical Equivalence Check
text: table juxtaposes R results computed Python reference values reported Tables 1–2 Benavides-Herrera et al. (2026). metrics directly comparable two implementations use different train/test splits: set.seed(4); sample() R seeds R’s Mersenne Twister, random_state=4 sklearn seeds numpy’s Mersenne Twister. two generators produce different random sequences integer seed, 152 test observations differ two runs. mathematical equivalence implementations verified KKT unit tests tests/testthat/, matching held-metrics. Test-set metrics — Boston Housing, 70/30 split. R rows: set.seed(4) R’s RNG. Python rows: random_state=4 numpy’s RNG (different test set). Splits equivalent; metric differences reflect different test observations, implementation errors. NA: reported paper.

(2026) py_m3_mape  <- 10.06        # Table 1, Model 3 — MAPE (%) py_m3_rmspe <- NA_real_     # Table 1, Model 3 — RMSPE (%) not reported py_m3_r2    <- 0.8959       # Table 1, Model 3 — R² py_m3_mse   <- NA_real_     # Table 1, Model 3 — MSE not reported  py_m1_mape  <- 10.29        # Table 2, Model 1 — MAPE (%) py_m1_rmspe <- NA_real_     # Table 2, Model 1 — RMSPE (%) not reported py_m1_r2    <- 0.8602       # Table 2, Model 1 — R² py_m1_mse   <- NA_real_     # Table 2, Model 1 — MSE not reported  cmp <- data.frame(   Model      = c("Model 3 — LS-SVR RMSPE (R)",                  "Model 3 — LS-SVR RMSPE (Python, Table 1)",                  "Model 1 — \u03b5-SVR MAPE (R)",                  "Model 1 — \u03b5-SVR MAPE (Python, Table 2)"),   `MAPE (%)`  = round(c(m3_mape,  py_m3_mape,  m1_mape,  py_m1_mape),  4),   `RMSPE (%)` = round(c(m3_rmspe, py_m3_rmspe, m1_rmspe, py_m1_rmspe), 4),   `R2`        = round(c(m3_r2,    py_m3_r2,    m1_r2,    py_m1_r2),    6),   `MSE`       = round(c(m3_mse,   py_m3_mse,   m1_mse,   py_m1_mse),   6),   check.names = FALSE )  knitr::kable(   cmp,   col.names = c("Implementation", "MAPE (%)", "RMSPE (%)", "R\u00b2", "MSE"),   align     = "lrrrr",   caption   = paste(     "Test-set metrics — Boston Housing, 70/30 split.",     "R rows: set.seed(4) with R's RNG.",     "Python rows: random_state=4 with numpy's RNG (different test set).",     "Splits are not equivalent; metric differences reflect different test",     "observations, not implementation errors.",     "NA: not reported in the paper."   ) )

title: Python Replication: Numerical Equivalence Check
text: observed gaps (e.g. R² 0.82 vs 0.90 Model 3) evidence implementation bug. Numerical investigation confirms three things: Non-equivalent splits dominant cause. set.seed(4) seeds R’s Mersenne Twister; random_state=4 seeds numpy’s Mersenne Twister. independent generators happen share API convention seed integer produce entirely different random sequences. 152 test observations selected different, metrics computed different data. linear solver factor. Replacing base::solve() MASS::ginv() (pseudoinverse via SVD, matching Python’s numpy.linalg.lstsq) changes predictions 3 × 10⁻¹² dataset. augmented system condition number κ ≈ 2 100, LU decomposition SVD agree machine precision. QP solver minor factor. osqp (ADMM) cvxopt (interior-point) solve QP different tolerances, producing slightly different dual variables. accounts 0.1–0.2 percentage points MAPE, multi-point gaps seen . correct way verify mathematical equivalence KKT conditions: unit tests tests/testthat/ check fitted models satisfy KKT stationarity, complementary slackness, feasibility conditions within solver tolerance, independently particular split.

title: tidymodels workflow with psvr
text: synthetic even-function dataset package README: y=2+x12+0.5x22+εy = 2 + x_1^2 + 0.5\,x_2^2 + \varepsilon, ε∼𝒩(0,0.12)\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\,0.1^2). Targets strictly positive construction (y>0y > 0).
code: set.seed(42) n   <- 200 x1  <- runif(n, -3, 3) x2  <- runif(n, -3, 3) y   <- 2 + x1^2 + 0.5 * x2^2 + rnorm(n, sd = 0.1) dat <- data.frame(y = y, x1 = x1, x2 = x2)

title: tidymodels workflow with psvr
code: set.seed(1) split <- initial_split(dat, prop = 0.75) train <- training(split) test  <- testing(split)

title: tidymodels workflow with psvr
text: Centre scale predictors RBF kernel operates standardised feature space.
code: rec <- recipe(y ~ x1 + x2, data = train) |>   step_normalize(all_predictors())

title: tidymodels workflow with psvr
text: cost (maps Γ\Gamma) rbf_sigma (RBF bandwidth σ\sigma) tune() placeholders; grid search explore combinations.

title: tidymodels workflow with psvr
code: wf <- workflow() |>   add_recipe(rec) |>   add_model(spec)

title: tidymodels workflow with psvr
text: search 15-point Latin hypercube cost rbf_sigma values evaluate fold MAPE. rbf_sigma search range set data-adaptively using rbf_sigma_psvr_data(), centred median pairwise distance normalised feature space. rmspe_lssvr() solves linear system, 75 fits complete seconds. Cross-validated MAPE candidate (lower better):
code: set.seed(2) folds <- vfold_cv(train, v = 5)  # Data-driven rbf_sigma range centred on median pairwise distance train_baked      <- rec |> prep() |> bake(new_data = train) rbf_sigma_custom <- rbf_sigma_psvr_data(train_baked |> select(-y))  wf_params <- extract_parameter_set_dials(wf) |>   update(rbf_sigma = rbf_sigma_custom)  tune_res <- tune_grid(   wf,   resamples  = folds,   grid       = 15,   param_info = wf_params,   metrics    = metric_set(mape) ) collect_metrics(tune_res)[, c("cost", "rbf_sigma", "mean", "std_err")] #> # A tibble: 15 × 4 #>        cost rbf_sigma  mean std_err #>       <dbl>     <dbl> <dbl>   <dbl> #>  1    0.25      4.78  42.0   1.07   #>  2    0.453     0.478 35.0   1.13   #>  3    0.820     1.78  38.2   1.11   #>  4    1.49     12.8   42.0   1.07   #>  5    2.69      0.178 34.0   1.13   #>  6    4.88      0.665 16.9   0.538  #>  7    8.83      2.48  30.4   1.17   #>  8   16         9.23  41.8   1.13   #>  9   29.0       0.248 14.7   0.472  #> 10   52.5       0.923  6.18  0.118  #> 11   95.1       3.44  18.8   0.897  #> 12  172.       17.8   41.8   1.22   #> 13  312.        0.344  4.05  0.322  #> 14  565.        1.28   2.99  0.0743 #> 15 1024         6.65  20.0   0.951

code: # extract_fit_engine() unwraps the parsnip/workflow layer to the raw psvr object psvr_fit <- extract_fit_engine(extract_workflow(final_fit)) print(psvr_fit) #>  #> LS-SVR with RMSPE loss  [psvr_rmspe] #>  #>   Kernel:        RBF (sigma = 1.28306) #>   Gamma:         565.294 #>   Training obs.: 150 cf <- coef(psvr_fit) # alpha: N dual variables; weight each training point in f(x) = sum_k alpha_k K(x_k, x) + b # b:     bias / intercept term # X_sv:  all N training inputs (LS-SVR has no sparsity — every training point contributes) cat(sprintf("b = %.4f  |  alpha range: [%.4f, %.4f]\n",             cf$b, min(cf$alpha), max(cf$alpha))) #> b = 12.6350  |  alpha range: [-18.4036, 21.9087]

title: When to Use Percentage-Error SVR
text: Standard regression models — including classical SVR — minimise absolute-error losses MSE MAE. losses scale-dependent: error 5 units treated identically whether target 10 10,000. many practical settings, however, matters relative error. forecast 5% 200 MPa concrete specimen equivalent practical terms one 5% 20 MPa specimen — even though absolute errors differ factor ten. psvr package provides four SVR variants optimise percentage-error losses directly: Model 1 (psvr_mape_rbf): ε\varepsilon-SVR MAPE loss Model 2 (psvr_mape_sym_rbf): symmetric-kernel extension Model 1 Model 3 (psvr_rmspe_rbf): LS-SVR RMSPE loss Model 4 (psvr_rmspe_sym_rbf): symmetric-kernel extension Model 3 article asks concrete question: dataset relative accuracy matters, models optimise percentage error directly outperform models optimise absolute error? article claim psvr universal replacement standard SVR. models require strictly positive targets best suited settings : () target spans wide range scales, (b) domain specifications stated percentage terms, (c) models trained different units datasets need compared fairly.

title: When to Use Percentage-Error SVR
code: library(tidyverse) library(tidymodels) library(psvr) library(modeldata) library(future)  tidymodels_prefer() theme_set(theme_bw())

title: When to Use Percentage-Error SVR
text: concrete dataset (Yeh, 1998) contains 1,030 observations concrete mixture compositions resulting compressive strength curing. target — compressive strength MPa — strictly positive spans roughly 2.3 82.6 MPa, range 35-fold. wide scale variation makes natural candidate percentage-error evaluation.  Compressive strength spans wide range, motivating relative error metrics. distribution right-skewed long upper tail. dataset? 35-fold range compressive strength means absolute-error loss weights high-strength specimens far heavily low-strength ones. model optimised MSE can achieve low aggregate error fitting high-strength region well relatively inaccurate low-strength concrete — may structurally critical regime.

text: models share single preprocessing recipe: centre scale predictors RBF kernel operates standardised feature space.
code: rec_base <- recipe(compressive_strength ~ ., data = train) |>   step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors())

title: When to Use Percentage-Error SVR
text: four models use RBF kernel. symmetry parameter = 1 (even symmetry) fixed engine argument — encodes assumption regression function satisfies f(𝐱)=f(−𝐱)f(\mathbf{x}) = f(-\mathbf{x}), reasonable centering via step_normalize. Hyperparameter search ranges psvr package supplies custom dials parameters appropriate defaults percentage-error models: svm_margin → margin_percentage(): default range [1, 20] percentage units — manual override needed. cost → cost_psvr(): default range [−2, 10] log₂ scale (~0.25 1,024) — manual override needed. rbf_sigma → rbf_sigma_psvr(): default range [−3, 1] log₁₀ scale. range adjusted using median-distance heuristic via sigma_heuristic() — see code .
code: spec_lm  <- linear_reg() |>   set_engine("lm")  spec_svm <- svm_rbf(cost = tune(), rbf_sigma = tune()) |>   set_engine("kernlab") |>   set_mode("regression")  spec_rf  <- rand_forest(mtry = tune(), trees = 500, min_n = tune()) |>   set_engine("ranger") |>   set_mode("regression")  spec_xgb <- boost_tree(   trees     = 500,   tree_depth = tune(),   learn_rate = tune(),   loss_reduction = tune() ) |>   set_engine("xgboost") |>   set_mode("regression") spec_m1 <- psvr_mape_rbf(   cost       = tune(),   svm_margin = tune(),   rbf_sigma  = tune() ) |>   set_engine("psvr")  spec_m2 <- psvr_mape_sym_rbf(   cost       = tune(),   svm_margin = tune(),   rbf_sigma  = tune() ) |>   set_engine("psvr")  spec_m3 <- psvr_rmspe_rbf(   cost      = tune(),   rbf_sigma = tune() ) |>   set_engine("psvr")  spec_m4 <- psvr_rmspe_sym_rbf(   cost      = tune(),   rbf_sigma = tune() ) |>   set_engine("psvr") # Compute data-driven rbf_sigma parameter from the normalised training predictors train_baked      <- rec_base |> prep() |> bake(new_data = train) rbf_sigma_custom <- rbf_sigma_psvr_data(   train_baked |> select(-compressive_strength) )  r <- dials::range_get(rbf_sigma_custom, original = FALSE) cat(sprintf("rbf_sigma range: [%.3f, %.3f]\n", r$lower, r$upper)) rbf_sigma range: [-0.433, 1.567]

text: Eight model specifications paired base recipe, yielding 8 workflows. workflow_map() tunes one Latin hypercube grid evaluates 10-fold CV full training set.
code: wf_set <- workflow_set(   preproc = list(base = rec_base),   models  = list(     lm           = spec_lm,     svm_rbf      = spec_svm,     rf           = spec_rf,     xgb          = spec_xgb,     m1_mape      = spec_m1,     m2_mape_sym  = spec_m2,     m3_rmspe     = spec_m3,     m4_rmspe_sym = spec_m4   ) ) |>   psvr_option_add(train_baked |> select(-compressive_strength))  wf_set # A workflow set/tibble: 8 × 4   wflow_id          info             option    result   <chr>             <list>           <list>    <list> 1 base_lm           <tibble [1 × 4]> <opts[0]> <list [0]> 2 base_svm_rbf      <tibble [1 × 4]> <opts[0]> <list [0]> 3 base_rf           <tibble [1 × 4]> <opts[0]> <list [0]> 4 base_xgb          <tibble [1 × 4]> <opts[0]> <list [0]> 5 base_m1_mape      <tibble [1 × 4]> <opts[1]> <list [0]> 6 base_m2_mape_sym  <tibble [1 × 4]> <opts[1]> <list [0]> 7 base_m3_rmspe     <tibble [1 × 4]> <opts[1]> <list [0]> 8 base_m4_rmspe_sym <tibble [1 × 4]> <opts[1]> <list [0]>

title: When to Use Percentage-Error SVR
text: Cross-validated MAPE 8 workflows (lower better). Shapes distinguish model families. Cross-validated MAPE — best configuration per workflow model minimises MAPE may minimise RMSE, vice versa. plot makes trade-explicit best configuration workflow.  MAPE vs. RMSE trade-best configuration workflow. Models lower-left quadrant best metrics.
code: rank_res <- rank_results(tune_res, rank_metric = "mape", select_best = TRUE)  rank_res |>   filter(.metric == "mape") |>   mutate(     wflow_id = fct_reorder(wflow_id, mean),     family   = case_when(       str_detect(wflow_id, "m1|m2|m3|m4") ~ "psvr",       str_detect(wflow_id, "svm")          ~ "SVM (kernlab)",       str_detect(wflow_id, "rf")           ~ "Random Forest",       str_detect(wflow_id, "xgb")          ~ "XGBoost",       TRUE                                  ~ "Linear"     )   ) |>   ggplot(aes(x = mean, y = wflow_id, colour = family, shape = family)) +   geom_point(size = 3) +   geom_errorbarh(     aes(xmin = mean - 1.96 * std_err, xmax = mean + 1.96 * std_err),     height = 0.25   ) +   scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set1") +   labs(     x        = "Cross-validated MAPE (%)",     y        = NULL,     colour   = "Model family",     shape    = "Model family",     title    = "Model comparison: cross-validated MAPE",     subtitle = "Points show best result per workflow; bars show ±1.96 SE"   ) rank_res |>   filter(.metric == "mape") |>   select(rank, wflow_id, mean, std_err, n) |>   rename(     Rank     = rank,     Workflow = wflow_id,     MAPE     = mean,     SE       = std_err,     Folds    = n   ) |>   mutate(across(c(MAPE, SE), \(x) round(x, 2))) |>   knitr::kable(caption = "Cross-validated MAPE — best configuration per workflow") best_mape <- rank_results(tune_res, rank_metric = "mape", select_best = TRUE) |>   filter(.metric == "mape") |>   select(wflow_id, mape = mean)  best_rmse <- rank_results(tune_res, rank_metric = "rmse", select_best = TRUE) |>   filter(.metric == "rmse") |>   select(wflow_id, rmse = mean)  tradeoff <- left_join(best_mape, best_rmse, by = "wflow_id") |>   mutate(     family = case_when(       str_detect(wflow_id, "m1|m2|m3|m4") ~ "psvr",       str_detect(wflow_id, "svm")          ~ "SVM (kernlab)",       str_detect(wflow_id, "rf")           ~ "Random Forest",       str_detect(wflow_id, "xgb")          ~ "XGBoost",       TRUE                                  ~ "Linear"     ),     label = str_remove(wflow_id, "^base_")   )  tradeoff |>   ggplot(aes(x = rmse, y = mape, colour = family, label = label)) +   geom_point(size = 3.5, alpha = 0.85) +   ggrepel::geom_text_repel(size = 3, max.overlaps = 20) +   scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set1") +   labs(     x        = "Cross-validated RMSE (MPa)",     y        = "Cross-validated MAPE (%)",     colour   = "Model family",     title    = "MAPE vs. RMSE trade-off",     subtitle = "Each point = best hyperparameter config for that workflow"   )

title: When to Use Percentage-Error SVR
text: results illustrate core argument: target spans wide range scales, models minimise percentage error directly tend achieve lower MAPE minimise absolute error — even latter strong general-purpose models like XGBoost Random Forest. advantage pronounced low-strength region concrete dataset, absolute-error models sacrifice accuracy fit high-strength observations. Targets can zero negative (percentage-error loss undefined) Settings targets scale absolute accuracy natural metric (e.g., measuring deviation fixed setpoint) large datasets (n>5,000n > 5{,}000), O(n2)O(n^2) kernel matrix becomes expensive — gradient-boosted trees scale much better

title: psvr — Percentage-Error SVR
text: psvr implements four support vector regression models derived unified mathematical framework percentage-error loss functions. Classical SVR minimises absolute-error losses (MAE, MSE), scale-dependent: error 1 unit negligible target 1 000 critical 2. psvr addresses optimising MAPE RMSPE directly, making well-suited forecasting tasks targets strictly positive relative accuracy matters. models require strictly positive targets (y > 0).

title: psvr — Percentage-Error SVR
code: # Development version from GitHub remotes::install_github("pbenavidesh/psvr")

title: psvr — Percentage-Error SVR
text: full worked example Boston Housing dataset — including 70/30 train–test split, feature standardisation, comparison linear baseline — see vignette("getting-started", package = "psvr").
code:
library(psvr)  # Synthetic dataset: even function (f(-x) = f(x)), targets strictly positive set.seed(42) n  <- 100 X  <- matrix(rnorm(n * 2), n, 2) y  <- 2 + X[, 1]^2 + 0.5 * X[, 2]^2 + rnorm(n, sd = 0.1)  tr <- 1:70;  te <- 71:100 X_tr <- X[tr, ];  y_tr <- y[tr] X_te <- X[te, ];  y_te <- y[te]  # Standardise features using training-set statistics (important for RBF) col_mean <- colMeans(X_tr);  col_sd <- apply(X_tr, 2, sd) X_tr_s   <- scale(X_tr, col_mean, col_sd) X_te_s   <- scale(X_te, col_mean, col_sd)  K <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = 1)  # Model 1 — ε-SVR with MAPE fit1  <- mape_svr(X_tr_s, y_tr, kernel = K, C = 0.5, eps = 5) pred1 <- predict(fit1, X_te_s)  # Model 2 — Symmetric ε-SVR with MAPE  (a = 1: even-function prior) fit2  <- mape_sym_svr(X_tr_s, y_tr, kernel = K, C = 0.5, eps = 5, a = 1) pred2 <- predict(fit2, X_te_s)  # Model 3 — LS-SVR with RMSPE fit3  <- rmspe_lssvr(X_tr_s, y_tr, kernel = K, gamma = 100) pred3 <- predict(fit3, X_te_s)  # Model 4 — Symmetric LS-SVR with RMSPE  (a = 1: even-function prior) fit4  <- rmspe_sym_lssvr(X_tr_s, y_tr, kernel = K, gamma = 100, a = 1) pred4 <- predict(fit4, X_te_s)  mape <- function(y, yhat) mean(abs(y - yhat) / y) * 100  cat(sprintf("Model 1 (MAPE e-SVR):       MAPE = %.2f%%
", mape(y_te, pred1))) cat(sprintf("Model 2 (MAPE sym e-SVR):   MAPE = %.2f%%
", mape(y_te, pred2))) cat(sprintf("Model 3 (RMSPE LS-SVR):     MAPE = %.2f%%
", mape(y_te, pred3))) cat(sprintf("Model 4 (RMSPE sym LS-SVR): MAPE = %.2f%%
", mape(y_te, pred4))) #> Model 1 (MAPE e-SVR):       MAPE = 3.59% #> Model 2 (MAPE sym e-SVR):   MAPE = 6.04% #> Model 3 (RMSPE LS-SVR):     MAPE = 3.76% #> Model 4 (RMSPE sym LS-SVR): MAPE = 5.79%

title: Create a kernel function — make_kernel
text: Returns closure K(xi, xj) suitable use four psvr model fitting functions. returned function accepts numeric vectors sign, required symmetric models (Models 2 4) evaluate K(xk, -xl).

title: Create a kernel function — make_kernel
code: make_kernel(   type = c("rbf", "linear", "polynomial"),   sigma = 1,   degree = 3L,   coef0 = 1 )

title: Create a kernel function — make_kernel
text: type Kernel type: "rbf", "linear", "polynomial". sigma Bandwidth RBF kernel, sigma > 0 (default 1). degree Integer degree polynomial kernel, degree >= 1 (default 3). coef0 Constant term polynomial kernel (default 1).

title: Create a kernel function — make_kernel
text: function K(xi, xj) xi xj numeric vectors length, returning scalar kernel evaluation.

title: Create a kernel function — make_kernel
text: three supported kernels : RBF: K(xi, xj) = exp(-‖xi - xj‖² / (2 * sigma²)) Linear: K(xi, xj) = xi · xj Polynomial: K(xi, xj) = (xi · xj + coef0)^degree RBF even-degree polynomial kernels satisfy Assumption 3 paper (kernel symmetry), making compatible symmetric models. linear kernel odd-degree polynomial kernels satisfy Assumption 3 used mape_sym_svr() rmspe_sym_lssvr().

title: Create a kernel function — make_kernel
code: K <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = 0.5) K(c(1, 2), c(3, 4)) #> [1] 1.125352e-07  K_lin <- make_kernel("linear") K_lin(c(1, 0), c(0, 1)) #> [1] 0  K_poly <- make_kernel("polynomial", degree = 2, coef0 = 1) K_poly(c(1, 2), c(3, 4)) #> [1] 144

title: Fit epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 1) — mape_svr
text: Solves quadratic program derived Theorem 1 Benavides-Herrera et al. (2026) via osqp. dual expressed variables u = [α; α*] ∈ R^{2N} :

title: Fit epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 1) — mape_svr
code: mape_svr(X, y, kernel, C, eps, tol = 1e-05)

title: Fit epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 1) — mape_svr
text: X Numeric matrix training inputs, one observation per row (N × p). y Numeric vector training targets, length N. Must satisfy y > 0. kernel kernel function created make_kernel(). C Regularization parameter C > 0. eps Insensitivity tube half-width ε ≥ 0 (percentage units, .e., tube ε% target). tol Threshold |βk| treated zero selecting support vectors free support vectors (default 1e-5).

title: Fit epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 1) — mape_svr
text: object class "psvr_mape", list components: beta Numeric vector non-zero dual differences βk (length equal number support vectors). b Numeric scalar bias term. X_sv Numeric matrix support vector inputs. y_sv Numeric vector support vector targets. kernel kernel function used. C regularization parameter C. eps ε value used. n_train Number training observations. p_train Number training features (columns).

title: Fit epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 1) — mape_svr
text: P = [Ω, -Ω; -Ω, Ω] (2N × 2N, upper triangular passed osqp) q = [y(ε/100 − 1); y(1 + ε/100)] Equality: [1ᵀ, −1ᵀ] u = 0 Box: 0 ≤ αk ≤ 100C/yk, 0 ≤ αk* ≤ 100C/yk solving, βk = αk − αk* support vectors |βk| > tol retained.

title: Fit epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 1) — mape_svr
code: X <- matrix(1:6, ncol = 2) y <- c(2.1, 3.8, 6.2) K <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = 1) fit <- mape_svr(X, y, kernel = K, C = 10, eps = 5) predict(fit, X) #> [1] 2.205002 3.990000 5.889998

what: Fit symmetric epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 2) — mape_sym_svr
title: Fit symmetric epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 2) — mape_sym_svr
text: Solves quadratic program derived Theorem 2 Benavides-Herrera et al. (2026) via osqp. symmetry constraint f(x) = ·f(-x) enforced replacing kernel Ks(xi, xj) = K(xi, xj) + ·K(xi, -xj).

title: Fit symmetric epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 2) — mape_sym_svr
code: mape_sym_svr(X, y, kernel, C, eps, a = 1, tol = 1e-05)

title: Fit symmetric epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 2) — mape_sym_svr
text: X Numeric matrix training inputs, one observation per row (N × p). y Numeric vector training targets, length N. Must satisfy y > 0. kernel kernel function created make_kernel(). C Regularization parameter C > 0. eps Insensitivity tube half-width ε ≥ 0 (percentage units). Symmetry parameter: 1 even symmetry f(x) = f(-x), -1 odd symmetry f(x) = -f(-x). tol Threshold |βk| treated zero (default 1e-5).

title: Fit symmetric epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 2) — mape_sym_svr
text: object class "psvr_mape_sym", list components: beta Numeric vector non-zero dual differences βk. b Numeric scalar bias term. X_sv Numeric matrix support vector inputs. y_sv Numeric vector support vector targets. kernel kernel function used. C regularization parameter C. eps ε value used. symmetry parameter. n_train Number training observations. p_train Number training features (columns).

title: Fit symmetric epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 2) — mape_sym_svr
text: dual variables u = [α; α*] ∈ R^{2N} : P = ½ · [Ks, -Ks; -Ks, Ks] osqp's ½ uᵀPu evaluates ¼ βᵀKsβ, matching −¼ coefficient Theorem 2. q = [y(ε/100 − 1); y(1 + ε/100)] (identical Model 1) Equality: [1ᵀ, −1ᵀ] u = 0 Box: 0 ≤ αk ≤ 100C/yk, 0 ≤ αk* ≤ 100C/yk (identical Model 1) Note: Ks = Ω + ·Ω* carries ½ factor . ½ lives P osqp's internal ½ produces required ¼ overall. Contrast rmspe_sym_lssvr() (Model 4), uses sym_kernel_matrix() returning ½(Ω + ·Ω*) = Ωs. kernel must satisfy Assumption 3 paper; see make_kernel().

title: Fit symmetric epsilon-SVR with MAPE loss (Model 2) — mape_sym_svr
code: X <- matrix(1:6, ncol = 2) y <- c(2.1, 3.8, 6.2) K <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = 1) fit <- mape_sym_svr(X, y, kernel = K, C = 10, eps = 5, a = 1) predict(fit, X) #> [1] 2.205002 3.990000 5.889998

Must satisfy y > 0. kernel kernel function created make_kernel(). gamma Regularization parameter Γ > 0. Symmetry parameter: 1 even symmetry f(x) = f(-x), -1 odd symmetry f(x) = -f(-x).

title: Fit symmetric LS-SVR with RMSPE loss (Model 4) — rmspe_sym_lssvr
text: YΓ = diag(y₁²/Γ, …, yN²/Γ). kernel must satisfy Assumption 3 paper (kernel symmetry): K(-xi, xj) = K(xi, -xj) K(-xi, -xj) = K(xi, xj). RBF even-degree polynomial kernels satisfy ; see make_kernel().

title: Fit symmetric LS-SVR with RMSPE loss (Model 4) — rmspe_sym_lssvr
code: X <- matrix(c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6), ncol = 2) y <- c(2.1, 3.8, 6.2) K <- make_kernel("rbf", sigma = 1) fit <- rmspe_sym_lssvr(X, y, kernel = K, gamma = 1, a = 1) predict(fit, X) #> [1] 2.769355 2.853120 2.886170

title: psvr 0.0.0.9003
text: New function rbf_sigma_psvr_data(): combines sigma_heuristic() rbf_sigma_psvr() single call, returning dials parameter data-driven search range centred median pairwise distance. New function psvr_option_add(): applies option_add() every psvr workflow (whose wflow_id matches m1|m2|m3|m4) workflow set simultaneously, replacing rbf_sigma parameter data-driven one. Replaces four individual option_add() calls typical workflows. Symmetric model specs (psvr_mape_sym_*, psvr_rmspe_sym_*): even symmetry (= 1L) now default engine argument. Calling set_engine("psvr") without = 1L now produces result — action required existing code already passed = 1L. four core fitting functions now emit warning N > 2000 remind users O(n²) kernel matrix may expensive. y > 0 input check four core fitting functions now reports number offending values minimum, making easier diagnose data issues.

title: psvr 0.0.0.9002
text: New function margin_percentage(): dials parameter epsilon tube half-width MAPE models, expressed percentage units default range [1, 20]. Replaces absolute-unit dials::svm_margin() 6 MAPE specs. New function sigma_heuristic(): returns median pairwise Euclidean distance predictor matrix — standard data-driven starting point RBF kernel bandwidth (Schölkopf & Smola, 2002). New function rbf_sigma_psvr(): dials parameter RBF kernel bandwidth finalize hook auto-sets search range using sigma_heuristic() training data available. Replaces dials::rbf_sigma() RBF specs. New function cost_psvr(): dials parameter regularisation parameter range [-2, 10] log2 scale (approx. 0.25 1024), wider dials::cost() accommodate larger values typically needed LS-SVR models. Replaces dials::cost() 12 specs. rbf_sigma_psvr(): removed non-functional finalize hook. search range must set manually using sigma_heuristic() option_add(). See ?rbf_sigma_psvr recommended workflow.

title: psvr 0.0.0.9001
text: Expanded parsnip integration 4 12 model specs following tidymodels pattern used svm_rbf(), svm_poly(), svm_linear(): psvr_mape_rbf(), psvr_mape_poly(), psvr_mape_linear() — Model 1 specs. psvr_mape_sym_rbf(), psvr_mape_sym_poly(), psvr_mape_sym_linear() — Model 2 specs. psvr_rmspe_rbf(), psvr_rmspe_poly(), psvr_rmspe_linear() — Model 3 specs. psvr_rmspe_sym_rbf(), psvr_rmspe_sym_poly(), psvr_rmspe_sym_linear() — Model 4 specs. Kernel parameters now tunable parsnip args mapped existing dials params: rbf_sigma → dials::rbf_sigma(), degree → dials::degree(), scale_factor → dials::scale_factor(). kernel closure built inside fit wrapper longer appears parsnip layer. old single-spec API (psvr_mape(), psvr_mape_sym(), psvr_rmspe(), psvr_rmspe_sym()) removed. Migrate replacing, example, psvr_rmspe(cost = tune()) |> set_engine("psvr", kernel = K) psvr_rmspe_rbf(cost = tune(), rbf_sigma = 1) |> set_engine("psvr").

title: psvr 0.0.0.9001
text: vignettes pkgdown articles updated new spec-based API. 12 new smoke tests (fit + predict) specs tests/testthat/test-parsnip.R. pkgdown articles reorganised three named groups: Get Started, Case Studies, Technical Notes.

title: psvr 0.0.0.9000
text: Initial development release.

title: psvr 0.0.0.9000
text: mape_svr() — epsilon-SVR MAPE loss (Model 1). Solves dual QP via osqp per-sample box constraints |βk| ≤ 100C/yₖ. mape_sym_svr() — symmetric epsilon-SVR MAPE loss (Model 2). Enforces f(x) = ·f(-x) replacing kernel symmetric kernel Ks(xi, xj) = K(xi, xj) + ·K(xi, -xj). rmspe_lssvr() — LS-SVR RMSPE loss (Model 3). Solves (N+1)×(N+1) linear system directly via base::solve(). rmspe_sym_lssvr() — symmetric LS-SVR RMSPE loss (Model 4). linear system Model 3 symmetrized kernel matrix Ωs = ½(Ω + ·Ω*). four models derived unified percentage-error loss framework; see Benavides-Herrera et al. (2026) mathematical proofs.

title: psvr 0.0.0.9000
text: make_kernel() — factory returning kernel closure type ∈ {"rbf", "linear", "polynomial"}. RBF even-degree polynomial kernels satisfy symmetry assumption (Assumption 3) required Models 2 4.

title: psvr 0.0.0.9000
text: Four parsnip model specifications use within tidymodels workflows: psvr_mape() — spec Model 1; hyperparameters cost svm_margin. psvr_mape_sym() — spec Model 2; hyperparameters cost svm_margin. psvr_rmspe() — spec Model 3; hyperparameter cost (maps Γ). psvr_rmspe_sym() — spec Model 4; hyperparameter cost (maps Γ). kernel (symmetric models) symmetry parameter engine arguments supplied via set_engine("psvr", kernel = ..., = ...). Hyperparameters map dials::cost() dials::svm_margin() compatibility tune_grid().

title: psvr 0.0.0.9000
text: 84 unit tests covering four models: kernel correctness, QP/linear-system solutions, support vector selection, bias recovery, predict consistency, input validation.
